title: Medu Vada (Savory Donuts)

ingredients.title: For the Batter
items: 2 cups whole urad dal (skinned black gram)
1 tsp rice flour (optional, for extra crispness)

ingredients.title: For Flavoring
items: 1 medium onion, finely chopped (optional, for Medu Vada with onion)
½ tsp black peppercorns, lightly crushed

ingredients.title: For Frying
items: Refined oil or sunflower oil (enough for deep-frying)

instruction.title: Step 1: Soaking & Grinding
steps: Wash the urad dal thoroughly and soak it in plenty of water for 4–5 hours.
Drain completely. In a wet grinder (preferred for authentic texture), grind the dal into a smooth, fluffy batter. Use as little water as possible — just enough to keep the grinder moving.
The batter should be thick, airy, and hold shape (like whipped cream consistency).

instruction.title: Step 2: Aeration Test (Chef’s Tip)
steps: Drop a small amount of batter into a bowl of water. If it floats, it has incorporated enough air — ensuring fluffy vadas.

instruction.title: Step 3: Mixing Flavors
steps: Add salt, rice flour (if using), ginger, chilies, curry leaves, pepper, cumin, and onion (if using). Mix well with your hand (this further aerates the batter).

instruction.title: Step 4: Shaping the Vada
steps: Keep a bowl of water nearby. Wet your palms slightly. Take a small portion of batter (golf ball size), flatten slightly, and make a hole in the center with your thumb — forming the classic doughnut shape.
Pro tip: Experienced chefs use a banana leaf or a plastic sheet lightly greased with oil to shape vadas before sliding them into hot oil.

instruction.title: Step 5: Frying
steps: Slide in the vadas gently, 3–4 at a time. Do not overcrowd.
Fry until golden brown and crisp outside, soft and fluffy inside — about 4–5 minutes, turning occasionally.
Remove and drain on absorbent paper.

instruction.title: Serving Suggestions (Restaurant-Style)
steps: Serve hot with coconut chutney, tomato chutney, and sambar.
For a luxurious restaurant touch, plate with a mini sambar bowl, a trio of chutneys, and a small garnish of fried curry leaves on the vadas.

chefTips: Grinder over Mixer: Always prefer a wet grinder over a blender for fluffier vadas.
No Excess Water: Adding too much water makes the batter heavy and vadas oily.
Uniform Oil Temperature: If oil is too hot, vadas will brown outside but stay raw inside. Too cold, they’ll soak up oil.
Banana Leaf Shaping: Traditional South Indian chefs often shape vadas on banana leaves for perfect roundness.
